mnemonics.title: Firewall Types Memory Trick
mnemonics.content: P-S-N: Packet (Packet Filter) → Stateful → NGFW (Next Generation) - increasing sophistication

mnemonics.title: DoS vs DDoS
mnemonics.content: DDoS = DoS with DISTRIBUTION - Multiple sources attacking one target

mnemonics.title: OSI Layers for Firewalls
mnemonics.content: P-3 (Packet Filter-L3), S-4 (Stateful-L3-4), P-7 (Proxy-L7) - Remember 3, 4, 7 numbers

acronyms.usage: Separated network segment for public services
Monitors and alerts about suspicious network activity
Detects and actively blocks threats in real-time
Maps private IP addresses to public IPs
Advanced firewall with app inspection and threat prevention
Encrypted tunnel for secure remote network access
Rules defining who can access network resources
Maps IP addresses to MAC addresses on local network

visualTips.visualization: Imagine a security checkpoint: Each packet stops at the firewall gate, gets its ID checked (source/dest IP, port), and either gets a pass (allowed) or is turned back (denied). Stateful inspection remembers who's already entered.

Think of DMZ like the reception area of a building. External visitors (internet users) can access reception (DMZ) but cannot go to internal offices (private network). Only authorized staff can move between areas.
Imagine thousands of people simultaneously calling one phone number (target) from different phones (botnet), making it impossible for legitimate callers to get through.

analogies.analogy: A firewall is like a security guard at a building entrance, checking IDs (packet headers) and deciding who can enter based on predefined rules.
Like a bouncer who not only checks your ID at the door but also remembers you've been allowed in and tracks your activity inside.
Like a concierge at a hotel who receives all your mail, opens it, and hands you only what you're allowed to see, hiding your identity.
A botnet is like a network of zombie employees at different branches, all following one evil manager's commands without their boss knowing.
Like someone secretly opening your letters at the post office, reading them, resealing, and sending them on - neither sender nor receiver knows.
A DMZ is like a showroom in a shopping complex - public can visit the showroom, but cannot access the warehouse or back offices.
